title: KNN Similarity Model
text:
The KNN model is a similarity model. For a team in a match, it finds historical team-match rows that look most similar, then summarizes what happened in those nearby games.
This gives the project a useful contrast:

OLS explains average goal changes with coefficients.
Ordinal logistic explains ordered win/draw/loss probabilities.
KNN asks: “What happened when past teams looked like this team in this matchup?”

title: KNN Similarity Model
text:
2 How It Works
KNN stands for K-Nearest Neighbors.
For each test row, the model:

Standardizes the model inputs so all features are on comparable scales.
Finds the 25 closest historical team-match rows.
Predicts goals by averaging the goals scored by those neighbors.
Predicts loss, draw, and win probabilities by counting neighbor outcomes.



Show code
features <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "knn_similarity_model_features.csv"))

if (nrow(features) > 0) {
  display_table(features, caption = "Features used to find similar team-match rows.")
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("KNN feature metadata is available after the KNN refresh."))
}

metrics <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "knn_similarity_model_metrics.csv"))

if (nrow(metrics) > 0) {
  metrics |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      rows_total = fmt_integer(rows_total),
      rows_train_evaluated = fmt_integer(rows_train_evaluated),
      rows_test_evaluated = fmt_integer(rows_test_evaluated),
      k_neighbors = k_neighbors,
      goals_rmse = fmt_number(goals_rmse, 3),
      goals_mae = fmt_number(goals_mae, 3),
      result_accuracy = fmt_percent(result_accuracy, 1),
      result_log_loss = fmt_number(result_log_loss, 3),
      result_multiclass_brier = fmt_number(result_multiclass_brier, 3),
      reference_accuracy = fmt_percent(reference_accuracy, 1)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "KNN model performance.")
} else {

text:

predictions <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "knn_similarity_model_sample_predictions.csv"))

if (nrow(predictions) > 0) {
  predictions |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      team,
      opponent,
      tournament,
      goals_for,
      pred_goals_for_knn,
      actual_result,
      predicted_result,
      pred_prob_loss,
      pred_prob_draw,
      pred_prob_win
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      pred_goals_for_knn = fmt_number(pred_goals_for_knn, 2),
      pred_prob_loss = fmt_percent(pred_prob_loss, 1),
      pred_prob_draw = fmt_percent(pred_prob_draw, 1),
      pred_prob_win = fmt_percent(pred_prob_win, 1)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Sample KNN predictions.", max_rows = 12)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("KNN predictions are available after the KNN refresh."))
}

title: KNN Similarity Model
text:
5 Interpretation
KNN is easy to explain because the prediction comes from comparable historical cases. That is also its limitation.
The model depends heavily on:

The features used to define similarity.
The number of neighbors.
Whether older matches should count the same as recent matches.
Whether rare outcomes, especially draws, are represented well enough in the neighbor set.

KNN is strongest as a comparison model. If it beats the simpler regression models, the feature set probably contains useful nonlinear patterns. If it loses, the added flexibility is not earning its keep.

The board uses three active layers:

Winner and outcome: an ensemble of the ordinal logistic result model and probabilities derived from the Poisson score grid.
Goals and likely score: the Poisson goals model produces expected goals; the score grid turns those expected goals into scoreline probabilities.
Totals: over 2.5 goals and both-teams-to-score probabilities come from the same Poisson score grid.

Cards and lineups are live-enrichment layers. They update when the football API returns player card history, event rows, or confirmed lineups for a fixture.

title: Matchday Prediction Board
text:
6 How To Read It

Predicted winner: highest ensemble probability. A draw remains possible for group-stage style matches.
Confidence: the model’s top probability bucket, not a guarantee.
Expected goals: model average score, not the most likely exact score.
Most likely score: the single scoreline with the highest probability in the Poisson grid.
O2.5: probability that total goals are 3 or more.
BTTS: probability that both teams score at least one goal.

text:
This report checks whether the first regression models are behaving well enough to interpret.
The checks focus on three practical questions:

Are the predictors repeating the same information?
Do residuals show patterns the model missed?
Is a count model a better shape for goals than simple linear regression?

4 Collinearity
Collinearity means two or more predictors are carrying nearly the same signal. It does not always ruin predictions, but it can make individual coefficients unstable.
VIF means variance inflation factor. A VIF near 1 is clean. Values above 5 deserve attention. Values above 10 are usually too high for coefficient interpretation.

title: Regression Diagnostics
text:
6 Poisson Checks
Poisson regression is a natural model for goals because goals are counts. The main issue is overdispersion.
Overdispersion means the observed goal counts vary more than a basic Poisson model expects. If this is large, the next goals model should consider a negative-binomial or paired-goal approach.

title: Regression Diagnostics
text:
7 Interpretation
These diagnostics are the gate before model comparison:

If VIF is low, coefficient directions are easier to trust.
If Durbin-Watson is close to 2, residuals are not strongly sequential in the ordered rows.
If heteroskedasticity appears, OLS standard errors should be treated carefully.
If Poisson overdispersion appears, expected-goal predictions may still be useful, but uncertainty intervals need a wider model.

text:

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
2 Model Iterations
The model section is organized around interpretable iterations. Each model card names the prediction target, explains the method, and lists the checks that determine whether the model is trustworthy.


2.1 1. Simple OLS Goals Model
Question: how many goals should a team score in one match?
The simple goals model starts with team strength difference:
\[
\text{Goals} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\text{Strength Difference} + \epsilon
\]
In words, beta_1 is the average change in expected goals for a one-unit increase in strength difference.
Why start here: OLS is easy to explain, fast to diagnose, and gives a clear benchmark before count models.
Main issues to check:

Goals are counts, so OLS can predict impossible values below 0.
Soccer scores are low and skewed, so residuals may not look normal.
Error variance can change by team strength or opponent strength.
Team-match rows are not fully independent because each match creates two rows.
Missing roster, injury, rest, venue, and market data can bias coefficients.



2.2 2. Poisson Goals Model
Question: how many goals should a team score when the target is a count?
The Poisson model predicts a positive expected-goals number:
\[
\log(\lambda)
= \beta_0
+ \beta_1\text{Team Elo}
+ \beta_2\text{Opponent Elo}
+ \beta_3\text{Expected Result}
+ \beta_4\text{Match Context}
\]
In words, lambda is expected goals. This is a better shape for soccer scoring than OLS because goals cannot be negative.
Main issues to check:

The basic Poisson assumes the mean and variance are tied together.
Soccer can be overdispersed, meaning the variance may be larger than the mean.
A negative binomial model may be needed if Poisson is too tight.
Forward-time validation matters more than random validation for future matches.

Open Poisson report


2.3 3. Three-Outcome Result Model
Question: what is the probability of win, draw, or loss?
The first three-outcome model is an ordinal logistic regression because the team result has a natural order:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
The model uses cumulative logits:
\[
\text{logit}\{P(\text{Result} \leq k)\}
= \theta_k - \eta
\]
with:
\[
\eta = \beta_1\text{Strength Difference}
+ \beta_2\text{Roster Strength}
+ \beta_3\text{Rest}
+ \beta_4\text{Venue}
\]
The output is three probabilities:
\[
P(\text{loss}), \quad P(\text{draw}), \quad P(\text{win})
\]
Main issues to check:

The proportional-odds assumption may be too strong.
Draws are not simply halfway between loss and win.
A multinomial logit may fit better if win, draw, and loss behave as separate categories.
Calibration matters more than raw accuracy for betting comparison.
Market odds should be the benchmark once API odds are available.



2.4 4. KNN Similarity Model
Question: what happened in historical matchups that looked similar?
KNN stands for K-Nearest Neighbors. The model finds the closest historical team-match rows, then averages those neighbors.
Outputs:

Expected goals from the average goals scored by similar teams.
Loss, draw, and win probabilities from the neighbor outcomes.
A flexible comparison against the regression models.

Main issues to check:

Similarity depends on which features are included.
Features must be standardized so one large-scale number does not dominate.
The choice of K controls the smoothness of predictions.
KNN can copy noise when the neighbor set is too small.
KNN is harder to explain with coefficients because it is case-based, not equation-based.

Open KNN report


2.5 5. Regression Diagnostics
Question: are the regression results stable enough to interpret?
Before the regression results are shown, the workflow checks collinearity, residual patterns, heteroskedasticity, and Poisson overdispersion.
Outputs:

VIF values for predictor overlap.
Durbin-Watson and lagged residual correlation.
Breusch-Pagan and Jarque-Bera diagnostic checks.
Poisson dispersion checks for goals.

Open diagnostics report


2.6 6. Fixture Predictions
Question: what does the current model predict for the 2026 fixture list?
The fixture table applies the trained local models to 2026 matches and shows expected goals, win/draw/loss probabilities, completed-fixture checks, and weather context.
Open predictions report


2.7 7. Matchday Board
Question: what should be checked right before kickoff?
The matchday board combines predicted winner, win/draw/loss probabilities, expected goals, most likely score, totals, lineup status, and yellow-card coverage in one table.
Open matchday board

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
3 Dictionary
Use this as the quick reference for acronyms and modeling terms that appear across the site.








Term
Plain-English Meaning
Why It Matters Here




Target variable
The thing the model is trying to predict.
For the goals model, the target is goals scored. For the result model, the target is win, draw, or loss.


Predictor variable
An input used to make the prediction.
Team strength, opponent strength, rest, venue, injuries, and odds can all be predictors.


Regression
A model that estimates how a target changes with predictors.
It gives a structured way to turn soccer information into expected goals or probabilities.


OLS
Ordinary Least Squares. A line-fitting method that chooses coefficients by minimizing squared prediction errors.
It is the first goals benchmark because it is easy to explain and diagnose.


Poisson regression
A regression model for count outcomes.
Goals are counts, so Poisson is a natural next model after OLS.


Lambda
The expected count in a Poisson model.
In the Poisson goals model, lambda is expected team goals.


RMSE
Root Mean Squared Error. A typical prediction miss, measured in the same units as the target.
If goals RMSE is 1.46, the model is often off by roughly 1 to 2 goals, with big misses punished more. Lower is better.


MAE
Mean Absolute Error. The average size of the prediction miss, ignoring direction.
Easier to read than RMSE because every miss is weighted evenly. Lower is better.


Coefficient
The estimated effect of one predictor, holding the other predictors fixed.
A positive strength coefficient means stronger teams are expected to score more, all else equal.


Residual
Observed value minus predicted value.
Residual plots show whether the model is missing patterns.


VIF
Variance Inflation Factor. A check for predictors that repeat the same information.
High VIF makes individual regression coefficients harder to trust.


Autocorrelation
A pattern where one residual is related to the residual before it.
Strong autocorrelation means model errors are not acting independently.


Durbin-Watson
A residual autocorrelation check. Values near 2 are usually cleaner.
It is a quick test for sequential structure in ordered model errors.


Heteroskedasticity
Uneven residual spread across predictor values.
It can make OLS standard errors too optimistic.


Overdispersion
When a count outcome varies more than a basic Poisson model expects.
If goals are overdispersed, a negative-binomial or paired-goal model may handle uncertainty better.


Logit
A transformation that converts a probability into log odds.
Logistic models use logits so probabilities stay between 0 and 1.


Odds
Probability of an event divided by probability of it not happening.
Model probabilities can be compared with betting-market odds after converting both to the same scale.


Ordinal logistic regression
A logistic model for ordered outcomes.
It fits loss < draw < win while producing probabilities for all three outcomes.


Multinomial logistic regression
A logistic model for three or more categories without assuming an order.
It is the backup if draws behave like their own special category rather than the middle of loss and win.


KNN
K-Nearest Neighbors. A model that predicts from similar historical examples.
It lets the project compare regression models against a flexible similarity-based method.


Accuracy
Share of predictions where the top predicted class matches the actual result.
Useful for a quick check, but not enough for betting because probability quality matters too.


Calibration
Whether predicted probabilities match real frequencies.
If the model says 60% win chance, well-calibrated teams should win about 60% of those cases.


Log loss
A probability score that penalizes confident wrong predictions.
Better than accuracy when the model outputs win/draw/loss probabilities. Lower is better.


Brier score
Average squared error between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes.
Another way to judge probability quality. Lower is better.


Test set
Data held out from fitting and used only to evaluate performance.
It helps estimate how the model performs on games it has not already seen.


Forward-time validation
Training on older games and testing on newer games.
This is closer to the real task: using past matches to predict future matches.


Overfitting
When a model learns noise in past data instead of repeatable signal.
A model can look good historically and still fail on new World Cup matches.


Data grain
What one row means in a dataset.
The main modeling grain is one team in one match. One match creates two team-match rows.


API
Application Programming Interface. A structured way to pull data from a provider.
API-Football is the live enrichment source once the baseline model is stable.


Ensemble
A prediction that combines more than one model.
The matchday winner uses ordinal result probabilities plus Poisson score-derived probabilities.


O2.5
Over 2.5 goals. The match has at least 3 total goals.
Common soccer totals view derived from the score grid.


BTTS
Both teams to score.
Another match-level probability derived from expected goals.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
4 Project Map

AboutData ExplorationModelingDataDisplay Standards


This project builds a reproducible data and modeling workflow for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
The workflow has four layers:

Data collection: free match history, Elo-style ratings, 2026 venues, weather, player metadata, and news signals.
Data storage: local files and DuckDB tables for modeling.
Modeling: interpretable models in R, with Python handling source refreshes and heavier data jobs.
Publishing: Quarto renders a shareable GitHub Pages site from the local analysis.



The starting modeling unit is one row per team-match. A single game creates two observations, one for each team.

The model suite has four core outputs:

Goals OLS: predicts goals scored.
Poisson goals: predicts expected goals as a count.
Ordinal logistic: predicts loss < draw < win.
KNN similarity: predicts goals and result probabilities from nearby historical matchups.

The reports show the model target, data grain, math, coefficients, predictions, and diagnostics.

if (nrow(goals_metrics) > 0 && nrow(ordinal_metrics) > 0) {
  model_rows <- data.frame(
    model = c("Goals OLS", "Ordinal result logistic"),
    target = c("team goals", "loss < draw < win"),

    primary_metric = c("Test RMSE", "Test accuracy"),
    value = c(
      fmt_number(goals_metrics$test_rmse[[1]], 3),
      fmt_percent(ordinal_metrics$test_accuracy[[1]], 1)

    context = c(
      paste("Mean test goals:", fmt_number(goals_metrics$mean_y_test[[1]], 3)),
      paste("Reference accuracy:", fmt_percent(ordinal_metrics$baseline_majority_accuracy[[1]], 1))
    )
  )
  if (nrow(poisson_metrics) > 0) {
    poisson_random <- poisson_metrics[poisson_metrics$validation_type == "random_80_20", ]
    poisson_forward <- poisson_metrics[poisson_metrics$validation_type == "train_through_2018_test_2019_plus", ]
    if (nrow(poisson_random) > 0) {
      model_rows <- rbind(
        model_rows,
        data.frame(
          model = "Poisson goals",
          target = "team goals",
          rows = fmt_integer(poisson_random$rows_total[[1]]),
          primary_metric = "RMSE",
          value = fmt_number(poisson_random$test_rmse[[1]], 3),
          context = if (nrow(poisson_forward) > 0) {
            paste("Forward-time RMSE:", fmt_number(poisson_forward$test_rmse[[1]], 3))
          } else {
            "Count-model benchmark"
          }
        )
      )
    }
  }
  if (nrow(knn_metrics) > 0) {
    model_rows <- rbind(
      model_rows,
      data.frame(

        target = "team goals and loss/draw/win",

Open matchday board

Distribution notes for deciding whether soccer features are counts, continuous variables, ordered outcomes, probabilities, or mixture processes.
Open distribution review

title: Goals Model
text:
This report documents the goals model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
Goals are non-negative counts. The distribution report compares count-model shapes used for soccer scoring.

title: Goals Model
text:
4 Elo Feature
For one team against one opponent, the expected result before kickoff is:
\[
p =
\frac{1}{1 + 10^{-\left(\text{Team Elo} + \text{Home Adv.} - \text{Opponent Elo}\right)/400}}
\]
Where:

Team Elo is the team’s pre-match rating.
Opponent Elo is the opponent’s pre-match rating.
Home Adv. is the home advantage adjustment.
p is the expected result on a 0 to 1 scale.

text:
7 Interpretation
The signs match soccer intuition:

pre_elo is positive: stronger teams score more.
opponent_pre_elo is negative: stronger opponents suppress scoring.
pre_match_expected_result is positive: teams expected to perform better score more.
listed_homeTRUE is positive: listed home teams score more.

title: Distributions
text:
1 Purpose
Each variable has an explicit statistical shape. Soccer data mixes low-count outcomes, ordered categorical results, probabilities, text/news counts, player availability flags, and continuous context variables.
The workflow lists plausible distributions, builds diagnostics, and filters models that do not fit the data or the betting question.

  observed <- goals_frame |>

    dplyr::mutate(observed_share = observed_rows / sum(observed_rows))
  poisson_ref <- data.frame(

    poisson_share = stats::dpois(seq(0, max(goals_frame$y_goals_for, na.rm = TRUE)), lambda = lambda_hat)

    ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "#157878", alpha = 0.82, width = 0.75) +
    ggplot2::geom_line(data = poisson_ref, ggplot2::aes(y = poisson_share), color = "#f2a65a", linewidth = 1.1) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(data = poisson_ref, ggplot2::aes(y = poisson_share), color = "#f2a65a", size = 2) +
    ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "Observed goals versus simple Poisson reference",

title: Distributions
text:
3 Candidate Distributions


Goals scoredPoisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated count models, bivariate Poisson, Skellam goal difference.Goals are low nonnegative counts.


Match resultOrdinal logistic, multinomial logistic, adjacent-category models, paired-score simulation.Useful for win/draw/loss odds comparison.


Player and news countsPoisson or negative binomial with exposure offsets where needed.Useful for goals, mentions, injuries, cards, and appearances.


ProbabilitiesBeta, Dirichlet, calibration curves, Brier/log-loss summaries.Useful once predictions are compared with market-implied probabilities.


Continuous contextNormal, t, lognormal, gamma, empirical splines, or transformed variables.Useful for weather, travel, rest, Elo differences, and player minutes.


Latent team strengthGaussian random effects, dynamic state-space ratings, hierarchical priors.Useful when teams have uneven schedule strength and sparse recent games.




Show code
candidate_models <- data.frame(
  data_family = c(
    "Team goals",
    "Goal difference",
    "Win/draw/loss",

title: Distributions
text:
4 Review Notes Captured
The public materials reviewed emphasize a few habits that fit this project well:

Use computing to build intuition, not just final answers.
Put distribution assumptions beside visuals so model choices are auditable.
Compare models on held-out or cross-validated performance, not training fit alone.
Teach the reader the data grain before showing the model.
Keep source code reproducible while making the public page readable.

Sources reviewed include Professor Justin Post’s personal/course site, his ST 558 course repository, his Shiny teaching app page, and the Distributome project linked from those teaching resources.
Reference links:
